does: This paper extends a neuro-symbolic causal framework by adding a meta-level layer that synthesizes and verifies formal rules from natural-language goals and safety principles. The approach uses large language models to decompose high-level objectives into candidate causes, consolidate semantics to eliminate redundancies, and translate them into first-order logic rules. A Rule Verification Engine then iteratively refines the formal rule theory through interaction with human experts, addressing goal misspecification by grounding rule synthesis in explicit safety and legal principles. The framework integrates structural causal models with deep reinforcement learning within a MAPE-K adaptive loop to enable explainable system behavior under distribution shifts while maintaining formal verification guarantees.
connects: This work builds on prior neuro-symbolic approaches that combine logical reasoning with neural learning, extending earlier research on causal reasoning in AI safety and formal verification of rule-based systems. It responds to well-documented challenges in reward specification and goal alignment, particularly the reward hacking problem where narrow optimization leads to unintended behaviors. The paper connects to literature on interpretable AI and causal inference, leveraging structural causal models previously applied to understanding confounding and interventions. It also engages with recent work on using large language models for program synthesis and formal specification, combining these techniques with domain expertise elicitation methods from human-in-the-loop AI development.
enables: This framework enables more robust development of safety-critical AI systems by automating translation between informal human objectives and formal specifications, reducing manual verification bottlenecks. It points toward scalable approaches for maintaining complex rule systems in dynamic environments where objectives may conflict or shift. The integration of LLM-based synthesis with formal verification opens pathways for semi-automated policy design in domains like autonomous systems, healthcare, and legal compliance where explainability and formal guarantees are essential. Future work could explore automated conflict resolution between synthesized rules, real-time goal refinement based on deployment feedback, and extension to higher-order causal reasoning for increasingly complex safety-critical domains.
